section.title: Equity and global health
section.intro: AI tools are trained on high-income-country data and perform best in those contexts. What that means for organizations working in LMICs.

resources.title: WHO: Ethics and Governance of Artificial Intelligence for Health
resources.author: World Health Organization
resources.description: WHO's framework for AI in health. Six guiding principles including equity, inclusiveness, and transparency, with specific attention to LMICs and data sovereignty.

resources.title: EVAH Initiative: Evaluating AI for Health in LMICs
resources.author: Gates Foundation, Novo Nordisk Foundation & Wellcome Trust
resources.description: $60 million initiative to build locally-led AI evaluation capacity in low- and middle-income countries. Communities affected by AI health tools evaluate whether those tools work for them.

resources.title: AI and Digital Health in the Health Systems of Developing Countries
resources.author: Frontiers in Digital Health
resources.description: Tools built and validated in high-income-country contexts frequently fail when deployed in LMICs. Training data, infrastructure assumptions, and evaluation frameworks don't account for conditions on the ground.

resources.title: Lancet Global Health Commission on AI and HIV
resources.author: The Lancet Global Health
resources.description: AI applications for HIV prevention, treatment, and surveillance in resource-limited settings. Documents where AI capability outpaces health system readiness.

resources.title: Building a Healthier Future: AI in Healthcare
resources.author: NAACP
resources.description: Three-layer governance model for health AI equity: bias audits (technical), transparency requirements (institutional), and community governance councils (population).

resources.title: Algorithmic Bias in Public Health AI
resources.author: Frontiers in Public Health
resources.description: Systematic exclusion of rural and minority populations from AI training datasets. The bias follows existing data infrastructure, which follows existing resource allocation.

resources.title: America First, Africa Last? Health Data Extraction
resources.author: PLOS Global Public Health
resources.description: Health data extraction from Kenya, Rwanda, and Uganda. Partnership agreements give US/European institutions access with minimal reciprocal benefit.

resources.title: WHO: Large Multi-Modal Models in Health
resources.author: World Health Organization
resources.description: WHO guidance on large language models and multimodal AI in health. Model performance drops sharply outside English.

resources.title: From Rationality to Relationality: Ubuntu as an Ethical Framework for AI
resources.author: Sabelo Mhlambi
resources.source: Carr Center for Human Rights Policy, Harvard
resources.description: Proposes Ubuntu – "I am because we are" – as a relational ethics framework for AI that centers community and interdependence, as an alternative to Western rationalist frameworks.

resources.title: HealthAI: The Global Agency for Responsible AI in Health
resources.author: HealthAI
resources.description: Geneva-based nonprofit advancing responsible AI in health. Operates a Global Regulatory Network (10+ countries) and Community of Practice (200+ institutions across 50+ countries).

resources.title: JHU Center for Global Digital Health Innovation
resources.author: Johns Hopkins Bloomberg School of Public Health
resources.description: Includes VectorCam: AI mosquito species identification trained on images from LMIC field conditions – built where it's used.

resources.title: AI Bias in Healthcare: Implications for Equity
resources.author: Nature Medicine
resources.description: How training data biases translate into clinical outcomes. AI diagnostic tools validated in high-income settings may miss conditions that present differently in other populations.

resources.title: Gates/OpenAI Horizon 1000
resources.author: Gates Foundation & OpenAI
resources.description: AI-assisted diagnostics and decision support deployed in 1,000 primary care clinics across sub-Saharan Africa.

resources.title: WHO European Region AI Health Readiness
resources.author: WHO Regional Office for Europe
resources.description: Only 8% of countries in the WHO European Region have dedicated health AI strategies.

resources.title: Large Multimodal Models: Boon or Burden for LMICs
resources.author: Rohit Malpani & Keymanthri Moodley
resources.description: Projected 10 million health worker shortage by 2030. AI is framed as the solution, but the analysis maps what AI requires (infrastructure, data, training, oversight) versus what LMICs have.

resources.title: The Costs of Connection: How Data Is Colonizing Human Life
resources.author: Nick Couldry & Ulises Mejias
resources.description: Data colonialism as analytical framework. Health data from LMIC populations training models owned by high-income-country companies follows historical resource extraction patterns.

resources.title: AI in Global Health: Opportunities and Challenges
resources.author: Lancet Digital Health
resources.description: AI in global health: diagnostic tools, surveillance, health system strengthening. Performance degrades when tools built in high-resource settings are deployed elsewhere.

resources.title: Informed Consent and AI: New Ethical Terrain
resources.author: BMJ
resources.description: When participant data is processed by AI tools – even for analysis – does the original informed consent cover that use? No settled answer.

resources.title: The Environmental Costs of AI
resources.author: MIT Technology Review
resources.description: Training a single large AI model can consume as much water as a small town uses in a year.

resources.title: Deploying Medical AI in Low-Resource Settings: A Scoping Review
resources.author: Frontiers in Digital Health
resources.description: 30 studies on deploying AI in LMICs. What matters most: stable systems, trustworthy data, and empowered professionals.
